title: "The Age of Europe - L'Età dell'Europa" - 12ª Conferenza annuale Italo-Britannica organizzata nei giorni 17, 18 e 19 settembre 2004 (presso la Certosa di Pontignano) - (Nota: è stata registrata solo la sessione inaugurale)
text: e, Chris, abbiamo creato un clima di Pontignano dentro la Commissione europea, che è abbastanza un raggiungimento. Allora, cercherò di rispondere alla vostra questione, Chris, non referendo quasi a tutto al campo in cui ho cercato di essere attivo, ma cercherò di rispondere in termini più generali. Allora, se guardiamo i ultimi 10 anni, l'Europa, che è spesso trattata di non aver messo in posto sufficienti reformi strutturali, e possiamo tutti sbagliare su questo, ha messo in posto 4 strutturali strutturali storiche di se stessa. È molto conosciuto che, solo in 10 anni, ci sia stato l'accedimento del mercato singolo, del mercato singolo, dell'enlargimento e della Costituzione. Questo è molto conosciuto, ma è troppo facilmente dimenticato. E non dovremmo dimenticare che ogni esercizio di questi obiettivi è stato pronunciato come quasi impossibile da molto dell'intelligenza in Europa, negli Stati Uniti e altrimenti, forse un o due anni prima, che è passato in posto punctuale. Ora, credo che sia anche normalmente sbagliato quanto queste 4 cose possano avere una relazione con il grande problema dell'economia europea e la sua competitività. E' in più di nessun dubbio che l'Europa non presta bene dal punto di vista della competitività, ma vorrei fare il punto che, è che questo possa essere in parte a causa della concentrazione delle energie politiche su questi 4 obiettivi, ma che, allo stesso tempo, avendo trasformato l'Europa in questo modo, per esempio, rendendo l'Europa un po' più simile all'U.S. come un'entità, può solo aiutare la crescita futuro e la competitività in Europa. È chiaro, per esempio, che la singola correnza ha portato con la correnza da indisciplina fiscale a disciplina fiscale. È ben possibile che, nel termine sottile, questo possa aver portato una crescita più bassa, ma credo che tutti aggrediremmo che questo sia un prequisito sottile per il sviluppo futuro. Anche la costituzione, solo il mercato, solo la correnza, l'enlargimento, la costituzione, anche la costituzione. Giuliano, che è la relazione tra la costituzione e la competitività? Non c'è una relazione. Ma è chiaro a tutti che una compagnia non può essere competitiva se ha un processo di decisione veloce e incerta. Questo non è molto diverso per la nostra nazione o per un set di nazioni. A livello che la costituzione porti un processo di decisione più racionali e veloci, credo che questo aiuterà la competitività d'Europa. Quindi, quando vedremo i ultimi 10 anni della storia europea, credo che confermerà che l'Europa non ha fatto abbastanza bene dal punto di vista della performance economica, ma probabilmente abbastanza la base per una performance economica più soddisfatto dal punto di vista dell'Europa. È chiaro, però, che non possiamo solo restare là e aspettare questa struttura fondamentale di trasformazione per portare a livello la dividenza in termini di crescita più alta e dell'implicamento e della competitività. Ci sono le politiche necessarie. E qui, Chris, per rispondere alla vostra domanda o al titolo di questa sessione, i successi e i setbacki in Europa over the past 10 years, what I would regard as a major problem which has emerged more and more clearly is a decoupling. A decoupling by member state between two things. The willingness to accept a somewhat greater integration and coordination Europe-wise, selectively, without exceeding, with greater attention paid at the same time also to subsidiarity, but to accept some greater coordination in certain area, one prerequisite, and the other one to be serious and tough in terms of domestic economic reforms. To simplify things exceedingly, I believe that we have seen an increasing decoupling in Europe between these two necessary prerequisites, in my view, in order to have a competitive Europe, not just an internal competition between one and the other. To simplify things, and at the risk of being unjust, certainly Germany and France have once again displayed a greater willingness to accept coordination, including in the constitutional debate, than, say, the UK or Spain. At the same time, there have been pretty recalcitrant in terms of domestic economic reforms. I believe we need both things if we want a competitive Europe, but there has been this increasingly visible decoupling. Now, I think we should ask ourselves the question, is there an inherent conflict between these two attitudes, both of which, in my view, are necessary for a competitive Europe? I believe there is not inherent conflict. Of course, as I said, a great deal of attention, and here we can only learn from the UK, has to be paid to subsidiarity. And in certain areas, we have in the last ten years brought about greater coordination, but some devolution to the national authorities. I can here mention perhaps the reform in the area of competition. But I don't see that there is an inherent conflict between these two attitudes. And I must say, Italy, for one, as I see it, has been rather more close to Germany and France in terms of acceptance of a greater degree of coordination, and, at least in principle, but also with some implementations ready towards economic reforms domestically. Now, in this context, I see a very substantial hope in the enlargement. We normally consider the effects of enlargement at constant policies. Enlargement will not keep policies constant. Enlargement will transform the policy trade-offs, I believe. And what I see in the new member states, most of them, all of them, is at the same time the willingness to have greater coordination and, for sure, a strong central enforcer of the rule of the European Commission and a tough policy of domestic economic reform. And here, again, to make one reference to Italy, with all the abstraction of these geometrical discussions about foreign politics, I believe that a country like Italy, with a government like the present government of Italy, should perhaps even intensify the efforts to become a reference point without discarding other friendships and alliances for this group of small and or new member states, which are really rejuvenating the European Union. My time is almost over. One important setback or insufficient progress is the explanation of Europe to Europe's citizens, in particular to the younger generations. And here I see, frankly, a monumental paradox. Why? We all know that the younger generations lose interest and love for Europe, especially because they have not lived through the things that Europe has been able to remove. And at the same time, if we think of it, Europe is the key ally of the future generations. Giuliano referred to the dimension of the future, as I see it. Europe is the main driver, the main introducer of a reference to the future in our societies and in our debates, both in terms of forcing us all to consider the future and in terms of forcing us to respect the future, consider the future. What would have been in each of our countries the policies, the politics, if we had not had the target of 1993 for the single market, of 1997, to bring down the deficit? Now Lisbon is a bit weak because it does not crucify countries similarly effectively to that. So if there has been a reference to the future, it has been most of the times brought by what the collective wisdom of member states and the commission have decided should be a future reference for Europe. And it has forced countries and societies to respect the future. And really, many of our countries were running with a beatitude of unawareness, deficits of 12-15% of GDP without there being any debate. This was so at the beginning of the last ten years, Chris. Now we make fights about whether it is 2.9 or 3.1. But the substantial effect of Europe is that it has forced upon domestic political processes the respect for our children, for the future generations, not to have them born with the burden on their shoulders of the public debt. So I think that it should be possible, and I'm delighted that President Barroso has assigned a special responsibility to Vice President Marco Wallstrom to make an effort to explain Europe. I think that those to whom we find it most difficult to transfer the message of Europe, that is the younger generations, are really those which we should be able to mobilize. Final note, I think in ten years the relevance of the commission for all its weaknesses has increased. May I conclude with a personal reference here. Ten years ago, believe it or not, when I was first appointed to Brussels, Italian journalists, non-Italian journalists, were totally uninterested in my person. It genuinely asked me the question, but Professor, why on Earth, on Earth have you accepted to become European Commission? Well, last July I had at least the great satisfaction that nobody asked me, in Italy or elsewhere, Professor, why do you appear not entirely happy of not being able to continue to work at the core of the European process? Thank you, Chris. The Council of Côme, intent on burying German sovereignty, there was Monsieur Mitterrand, there was Jacques Delors, and that they had a vision of Europe and they were able to explain it to people and they were able to infuse people. And that today the generation of political leaders have no vision, all have an existential problem  with countries membership of the European Union. I wonder if you think that is true and I'd be very interested in your reflections on the other issue as well. Mario, would you like to start? I think we should try to create enthusiasm through concrete facts. And I believe that both your questions, Chris, are linked. Can I start from the last one? Conventional narrative about Europe. I think that Mitterrand called Delors era was a great one. But I think it was largely responsible for some of the subsequently intervened problems. Many of the things that Neil has done and talked about this evening are related remedies to an excess of vision relative to administration. And this has contributed to impoverishing the vision about Europe in our citizens. So I spoke earlier of a decoupling. I think here there is another decoupling. I think we should try to bring vision and administration hand in hand. Maybe I'm too pragmatic. Maybe I'm too British. I'm sorry for that. But, you know, having been in charge of the single market first and competition, then how can one avoid these influences? And this links back to the issue of explanation. When will we be able to bring to European citizens the notion that the commission employs a staff which is smaller than the city of Rotterdam does? And one third of it is related to translations and interpretations. So I think there is a closer link between vision and administration than we normally see. As to your first question, is there or not still an ideological divide? Well, here I have a personal, very strong conviction. I think it's profoundly wrong to reason in the following way. Low turn-out rates to the European elections, which is a fact. Hence there is a crisis in the politics of Europe. Hence let's try to make the politics of Europe as similar as possible to national politics. In my view, the crisis is a much wider one of the political process and of the democratic process of participation to the elections at the national level as well as the European level. And I'm glad that the Constitution did not go as far as many would have liked it to go in terms of mimicking the play of domestic politics. I think that the European Commission of course should have a president politically connotated with some involvement of the European Parliament in the selection of the president, but I think it would be the end of the European construction if we had, as many would like to see, a commission that is an executive, which is a centre left one or a centre right one. Can you just imagine what the reaction in the different member states, depending on their current political majorities, would be to decisions of enforcing the law in that member state if the colour of the commission is different from the one of the member state? So, I think it would be a profound mistake. Does this mean that the European Commission is not a political body? No, in my view. But in a sense, it is the party of integration, which can have more right-wing, more left-wing connotations. I think it's very important to have had an input. I mean, I think one of the problems of some rights in our member states recently arriving to power is the erroneous belief that because there was a predominantly majority of the left in the European Council in a previous age, then the European Union is a product of the left. I think that much of the European Union is a product of conservative thinking. The single market is largely the product of Mr. Thatcher. The single currency is largely the product, political speaking, Chancellor Call. And I love it when I see in the Commission that we have different positions not based on affinity to this or that political family. But if anything, or flag, if anything, more linked to the portfolio for which we are responsible, which is a logical, a rational assignment of responsibility. So I'm sure I did not answer to your question. Maybe I used enthusiasm after having said that we should drop it. But in terms of ideological divide and the political play at the European Union level, I firmly believe that the biggest mistake would be to just to try to make it identical to the domestic level. And of course here too explanation is important. The commissioners are faceless Eurocrats. Of course we are faceless. Of course we are Eurocrats. But we lack legitimacy. Mention to me one national government in Europe, not the US, where ministers, if I can for a second, presumptuously assimilate commissioners to ministers, undergo an individual written and oral scrutiny by the parliament. Of course one can say that there has been before that a parliamentary election, but we know this is not the case for all ministers. And at any rate they were not elected to be ministers. They have not gone through this individual scrutiny. This is again one myth that obviously we have not been able to dispel. And it's ludicrous to see people still convinced that the European parliament lacks any power when on the 15th of March, the edes of March of 1999, Neil, myself and other 18 colleagues went back home at midnight as resigned commissioners. By the way I was very much impressed that that evening for the first time I saw a 30 minutes cover. Thanks. Thank you. Mario. I thought the question was to Neil. Mario, you will give the same answer in a 30 minutes. Perfect substitutability. Well, Minister Baldassari, the European model is for one generation. My view is that the inertial European model, the de facto configuration in most of our member states, would be for one generation. The premium to the present relative to the future. It is the effort of the European Union to persuade member states to modify their de facto model into one which pays more attention to the future. And in this abstract and so pleasant academic environment in which we find ourselves this evening of course the future does bring us to the notion of investment and therefore to the metal which you reminded linking that metal to a rule have not mentioned that rule now. There is no question that in economic terms you know this better than anybody else. What is public or private expenditure for the future is more worthwhile than what is public or private consumption. But as you said yourself it's difficult then to identify what precisely is investment. And I was struck by a comment of our colleague Joachim Ammunia at a recent seminar in Italy where six members of the Italian government were present and Ammunia afterwards noted that each of the minister had excellent reasons to say that his expenditure I was going to say his or her but minister Moratti was not there on this occasion but probably with very good justification she would say the same. So that's why but so just a reference to the debate Neil and I and others had when the stability pact was created my position in the commission in 1997 was that we should pay attention to this distinction but we should also rush up to establish the methodology, the verification to assess what is what. Very last question. Perhaps you can be quite brief. Very short. Mario Monti said that
